title: Read a Medicine Label
summary: Read an over-the-counter medicine label to understand dosage, warnings, and ingredients.
learnerPromise: You will be able to find and understand the key information on an over-the-counter medicine label before taking it.
whyItMatters: Medicine labels contain important details about how much to take, how often, and what to avoid. Understanding these details helps you use medicine more safely.
realLifeUses: Buying pain reliever at a pharmacy and choosing the right product for your symptoms
Checking if a cold medicine is safe to take with your existing prescriptions
Figuring out the correct dose and timing for a fever reducer
Identifying an ingredient you are sensitive to before taking a new medicine
materialsNeeded: One over-the-counter medicine box or bottle (new, unopened)
Optional: magnifying glass or phone camera zoom for small text
result: You can locate and explain the active ingredients, dosage instructions, and key warnings on a medicine label.
youWillLearn: Locate the active ingredients and their purposes on a Drug Facts label
Read and follow dosage instructions by age or weight
Identify warning labels about drowsiness, allergies, or pregnancy
Check the expiration date and storage instructions
miniChallenge: Pick one over-the-counter medicine in your home and read its entire Drug Facts label. Write down three key facts you learned from it.
steps: Find the Drug Facts panel on the medicine package.
Read the active ingredients section and note what each one does.
Check the uses section to confirm this medicine matches your symptoms.
Read the warnings section, including allergy alerts and when not to use it.
Review the directions for the correct dose based on age or weight.
completionCriteria: You can point to and explain the active ingredients on the label
You can state the correct dose and at least one important warning
You can explain why reading the label matters before taking medicine
commonProblems: Some labels use very small print that is hard to read
Similar-sounding products may have very different ingredients
It can be confusing when multiple medicines contain the same active ingredient
tips: Ask a pharmacist to walk you through a label the first time if the text is overwhelming
Compare two similar products to see which ingredients differ
Keep a list of ingredients you are sensitive to and check every new medicine against it
helpBoundary: Ask a pharmacist or doctor before taking medicine if you are unsure about the dosage, have existing health conditions, take prescription medications, or are pregnant.
